summary: Fowler argues that companies should buy packages for utility (non-differentiating) business processes and build custom software for strategic advantages. The critical mistake is heavily customizing packages, which creates massive technical debt because packages lack proper development infrastructure like version control, testing, and deployment pipelines. This leads to upgrade nightmares—Gartner estimated $500 billion to bring corporate systems current. Instead, companies should either accept the package's way of working, use APIs to build custom interfaces alongside packages, or recognize that package customization platforms are poor development environments that cost far more than expected.
key_points: Buy packages for utility processes but accept their workflows; build custom for competitive advantage
Most packages lack 'deliverability'—version control, testing, deployment pipelines—making large customizations brittle
Package customization creates hidden costs that often exceed building custom software from scratch
Customizable packages are effectively another language/platform with the same adoption costs but worse hiring prospects
Prefer single-vendor solutions over best-of-breed to reduce integration complexity for utility functions
money_quotes: If the business process you are supporting is part of your competitive advantage you should build custom software, if not you should buy a package and adjust your business process to fit the way the package works.
The fact is that most packages aren't designed in such a way as to make customization really viable, at least not on a significant scale.
Recently a colleague came across a package customization that ran to 300KLOC of customization code.
The real cost is how much money has been wasted in customizations that weren't worthwhile or could have been cheaper with a purely custom route.
We must remember that many of these customizable packages are, in effect, another language or platform.
The nature of much package customization work deters able people, which makes it even harder to find good people.
Package environments usually provide a very poor platform for software development.
key_insight: Package customization appears cheaper than custom development but creates compounding technical debt because packages fundamentally lack the tooling and practices needed for sustainable software development at scale.
